history.notes: Somatic exercises for nervous system regulation are exploding on TikTok in 2026 under the 'neurowellness' trend. NEUROFIT is the main competitor at $15/mo but has limited free content and a clinical feel. Opportunity for a warmer, guided-video-first app with shorter daily resets (2-5 min somatic routines) targeting stressed millennials and Gen Z. Monetization via premium routine packs and $4.99/mo subscription. Buildable in SwiftUI with video player, timer, and streak tracking.

GO: Strong growing trend (top wellness trend of 2026 across multiple sources), only 5 dedicated iOS competitors with the leader at just 665 ratings, and a clear gap — the top competitor (Somatic Exercises by Thinkabout, 298 ratings) has 1-star reviews complaining about NO video or sound, just text instructions. Video-first guided somatic routines is a validated unmet need.

demandSignals: Somatic exercises and nervous system regulation identified as top wellness trend of 2026 by Dr. Axe, Glimpse Top 40, Smart Health Clubs, Active Wellness, and Sanctuary Wellness Spa
TikTok creator The Workout Witch (5M+ followers) drives massive awareness for somatic exercises; #somaticexercise is a trending hashtag
r/SomaticExperiencing active Reddit community with users discussing somatic exercises for nervous system dysregulation and burnout
Major fitness platforms (Peloton, Gymshark, Planet Fitness, Anytime Fitness) all publishing somatic exercise content — confirms mainstream crossover beyond niche therapy
marketSize.notes: Neurowellness market projected at $48.63B in 2026 with 31% CAGR. Multiple articles call 2026 'The Year of Body-First Healing.' Google Trends shows 'nervous system regulation' at peak interest. This is not a fad — it has institutional support from fitness chains and health publications.

competitors.identifiedGap: The #2 app (Somatic Exercises by Thinkabout) has 1-star reviews complaining about NO video or sound — users must read text instructions between exercises. Users explicitly ask for video demonstrations. NEUROFIT is clinical/data-heavy at $20/mo, leaving room for a warm, approachable, video-first app at a lower price point ($4.99-6.99/mo).
competitors.notes: Only 5 dedicated somatic exercise apps on iOS. The leader (NEUROFIT) has just 665 ratings — this is a young, unsaturated market. No app has crossed the 1000-rating threshold, meaning no dominant incumbent. The gap for guided-video-first content with a friendly UX is clear and validated by negative reviews.

monetization.mrrMath: Conservative: 800 downloads/mo (growing trend, low competition) * 6% trial-to-paid conversion * $6.99/mo = ~$335/mo Month 1, scaling to $500-800/mo by Month 3 as ASO compounds. Aggressive: 1500 downloads/mo * 8% conversion * $6.99/mo = ~$839/mo. Undercut NEUROFIT ($20/mo) and Somatic Exercises ($15/mo) while offering better content quality.
monetization.notes: Subscription is clearly viable — all top competitors use it. The category supports $6.99-19.99/mo pricing. Positioning at $4.99-6.99/mo undercuts the market while the video-first content justifies the price vs. text-only competitors. Free tier with 2-3 routines drives organic growth and reviews.

risks: Need to produce or source quality short-form somatic exercise videos (2-5 min each)
Video hosting/streaming adds to storage costs but can use on-device bundling for v1
technicalFeasibility.notes: Core stack: SwiftUI video player (AVKit), countdown timer, streak/calendar tracking, simple onboarding flow. No expensive APIs — all content is pre-recorded video. User data via Firebase/Supabase (free tier sufficient). HealthKit integration optional for v2. Standard iOS permissions only (notifications for reminders). Well within 1-2 week build timeline.

concerns: Must avoid medical/therapeutic claims (Guideline 5.1.1) — position as 'exercise and wellness' not 'treatment'
Use language like 'may help with relaxation' not 'treats anxiety/PTSD'
appStoreRisk.notes: Multiple somatic exercise apps already live on the App Store (NEUROFIT, SomaShare, Somatic Exercises, SomYoga, Somatic Health) — this category is clearly approved. As long as we frame it as guided movement/exercise rather than medical intervention, risk is low. No HealthKit dependency in v1 avoids additional review friction.
